name: Peter Yang
summary: Asked builders to compare the best interface/process for switching between Claude and Codex, highlighting workflow ergonomics as a key bottleneck in multi-agent coding.

name: Nan Yu
summary: Pointed out that startup survival rates have held up surprisingly well despite a larger number of funded startups, signaling stronger-than-expected ecosystem resilience.

name: Thariq
summary: Emphasized specs as a leverage point and promoted Agent SDK as the easiest way to build agents, reinforcing a specs-plus-SDK default workflow.

name: Ryo Lu
summary: Shared a timing signal that the space is still early but clearer now, suggesting AI-native software UX is moving from experimentation toward repeatable patterns.

name: Garry Tan
summary: Open-sourced his gstack CLI toolset, signaling that founder-grade internal operator stacks are increasingly being externalized as reusable infrastructure.

name: Matt Turck
summary: Questioned whether AI-era products can build social-platform-level network effects, focusing on long-term defensibility via flywheels and data loops.

name: Zara Zhang
summary: Noted that AI power users are often busier and more stressed, because productivity gains expand possible work faster than human prioritization capacity.

name: Dan Shipper
summary: Shared a bullish position on Figma as a thesis that incumbent SaaS platforms can adapt to AI and create value instead of being displaced.

name: No Priors
summary: Karpathy argues the frontier has shifted to orchestrating parallel agents and removing humans from verifiable optimization loops, with digital workflows compounding faster than physical-world robotics.
